# FRAMEWORK PDF CAPTIONS — afg-drought 2026-04-04
# framework_doc: https://reliefweb.int/report/afghanistan/afghanistan-anticipatory-action-framework-drought-2026
# pages: 12 · model: sonnet · via: claude headless
# Auto-generated descriptions of the PDF's visuals (maps/charts/tables). PUBLIC.
------------------------------------------------------------
--- page 1 ---
Main point: Cover/executive summary — Afghanistan Drought AA Framework (March 2026), two-window trigger system backed by CERF ($12M) + AHF ($10M).

Trigger Mechanism Statistics table:
- Window A (forecast-based): return period 6.14 years, activation probability 16%, years activated: 1985, 2000, 2001, 2004, 2006, 2008, 2018
- Window B (observation-based): return period 3.91 years, activation probability 26%, years activated: 2000, 2001, 2004, 2006, 2008, 2012, 2016, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2025
- Analysis years: 1984–2024

Overall stats box:
- Overall return period: 3.91 years
- Overall probability of activation: 28%
- Average total spending per year: $3.07M

Financing: CERF $12M + AHF $10M = $22M total; two AA frameworks both backed by pooled funds.
Endorsed by ERC on 04 April 2026; approved by RC/HC on 17 March 2026.
Valid 2026–2027.

--- page 2 ---
Main point: Action plan narrative — modalities, seasonality calendar, and drought context for Afghanistan.

Afghanistan seasonal calendar figure (based on FEWSNET):
- Rows: Winter wheat planting | Spring wet season | Poppy harvest | Wheat and barley harvest | Winter wheat planting | Second season harvest (rice & maize)
- Peak risk for spring flooding band shown spanning roughly Feb–Apr
- Trigger moments marked: Window A activation in March; Window B activation in April/May

Drought context: Between 2020–2025 Afghanistan experienced repeated droughts; groundwater levels dropped up to 30m. In 2025 drought affected ~3.4M people in 12 provinces across 65+ districts. Previous CERF allocations averaged $12.3M with ~5-month response window (April–August/October).

MPCA focus: CERF 50% on multi-purpose cash; AHF funds international NGO partners.

--- page 3 ---
Main point: Two-window trigger design — forecast (Window A, March) and observation (Window B, April) — with two-stage timeline diagrams.

Two timeline diagrams showing publication/validity windows:
Diagram 1 (Window A — forecast): Observations published in Feb, valid through Mar-May → trigger decision point at Mar/Apr
Diagram 2 (Window B — observation): Observations published in Apr, valid for Apr–May → trigger decision point at Apr/May

Trigger thresholds (table on following page, referenced here): composite drought indicator combining Environmental metrics, seasonal rainfall forecasts, snow cover, and vegetative health metrics against historical return-period thresholds.

AA Group formed 2024; members include FAO, IOM, OCHA, UNFPA, UNICEF, UN Women, WFP. Sub-national coordination via OCTs in Northern and North-Eastern regions.

--- page 4 ---
Main point: Trigger thresholds table — four indicators across two windows with data sources, evaluation timing, return periods, and weights.

Table — Window indicators:
| Window | Indicator | Data Source | Evaluation time | Return period threshold |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| A (forecast-based) | Forecasted precipitation Mar–Apr–May | ECMWF SEAS5 | Early March | 1-in-6-year RP |
| B (observation- and forecast-based) | Composite drought indicator (CDI) — composition of indicators in table below | Multiple | Early April | 1-in-4-year RP |

Window A threshold: ECMWF SEAS5 forecasts precipitation for MAM to be below 129mm (1-in-6-year rainfall deficit).
Window B threshold: Optimized combined drought indicator (CDI) exceeds 0.503 threshold in April → 1-in-4-year RP.

CDI component indicators and weights:
- Agricultural Stress Index (ASI): FAO; end of March (Dekad 3); weight 20.6%
- Vegetation Health Index (VHI): FAO; end of March (Dekad 3); weight 19.2%
- Combined observed + forecasted precipitation: ECMWF ERA5 + SEAS5; forecast Apr–May (issued April); weight 27.4%
- Snow cover: ECMWF ERA5-LAND; March; weight 20.5%
- 1-metre soil moisture: ECMWF ERA5; end of March; weight 12.1%

Combined overall return period for full allocation: ~1-in-4 years.
AOI: 5 monitored provinces (merged cluster).
Allocation not split across Window A and B — full allocation triggered by either window's threshold.

--- page 5 ---
Main point: Activation protocol table — who does what, when, and how, from monitoring through fund disbursement.

Activation protocol table:
| When | Who | What | How |
|------|-----|------|-----|
| Beginning of March or April | CHD | Monitors indicators for Window A / B | ECMWF SEASS portal |
| When trigger threshold is reached | CHD | Notifies OCHA country office and HQ, Afghanistan AA Group, CERF and AHF | Email |
| " | OCHA CO | Notifies CERF secretariat — will inform HC/HCT of activation | Email |
| " | CERF | Sends out approval letter authorizing agencies to use all funds | Email |
| Once trigger notification is received | AHF | — | — |
| " | FAO | Conduct quick ground-truthing through field presence | Technical field work |
| " | OCHA CO, AHG | Determine final geographical prioritization to triage the target districts | Meeting |
| Once target districts are selected | UN Agencies and NGOs | Implementation of AA activities per the framework and approved projects | Direct implementation with partners |

--- page 6 ---
Main point: Geographic prioritisation — five priority provinces identified; map shows spatial distribution across Afghanistan.

Map: Afghanistan with 23 prioritised districts highlighted across 5 provinces:
- Balkh (north): 1 district
- Faryab (north-west): 11 districts
- Jawzjan (north): 5 districts
- Sar-e-Pul (north): 5 districts
- Badghis (west): 1 district

Priority province selection criteria:
- Rain-fed agricultural land (more vulnerable to drought than irrigated land)
- Average yearly NDVI anomalies (2020–2023) and drought impact in 2025
- Provinces with strong correlation in historical drought events
- 23 prioritised districts total; 11 in Faryab, 5 in Jawzjan, 5 in Sar-e-Pul, 1 in Balkh, 1 in Badghis

Within-province district prioritisation based on: 2026 HNRP vulnerability data, food insecurity, nutrition, agriculture, livelihoods, health, water access. Targeting top 10–15 most vulnerable districts per province using WFP hotspot monitoring.

--- page 7 ---
Main point: Planned activities — objectives, activity list, implementation timeline, and gender/inclusion provisions.

Implementation timeline figure:
- Rows: Rainy season | Peak drought impact | Trigger moments | Window A implementation | Window B implementation
- Window A trigger: March (marked A); delivery of assistance band spans roughly Apr–Aug
- Window B trigger: May (marked B); delivery of assistance band spans roughly May–Sep/Oct
- Rainy season: winter months
- Peak drought impact: summer (Jun–Sep)

Objectives (pre-agreed actions to mitigate impacts):
- Enable informed decisions; safeguard agricultural production; avoid yield losses; avoid deteriorating food security; sustain livelihoods; ensure safe water; reduce disease outbreaks; reduce morbidity/mortality; reduce drought-related protection risks.

Activities:
1. Communicating risks / early warning messaging (via AAPWG / community radio)
2. Providing in-kind assistance (livestock protection inputs, emergency medical, dignity/hygiene supplies, therapeutic nutrition, NFIs for water storage, UNFPA)
3. Providing sectoral cash / multi-sectoral cash and integrated mixed modalities (MPCA key; 20% top-up for women-headed HH and HH with disability; GESI-aligned)
4. Facilitating access to services (mobile health clinics, maternal health, nutrition screening, referrals for protection)

--- page 8 ---
Main point: CERF funded activities — detailed agency/sector breakdown with people targeted and budgets; AHF funded activities summary.

CERF funded activities table:
| Agency | Sector | Activities | People Targeted | Budget |
|--------|--------|-----------|----------------|--------|
| FAO | Food Security-Agriculture | Cash+ livestock protection, PPR vaccines, Dewormer + CCNF; Cash for work (water conservation-rehabilitation); Ground Truthing | 85,120 | $3,250,000 |
| UNFPA | Protection | Distribution of Dignity Kits to Women of Reproductive Age and adolescent girls; Distribution and Replenishment of MHM kits to adolescent girls; Conducting orientation session for health service providers on PSEA; Dissemination of early warning messages | 15,069 | $375,000 |
| UNFPA | Health-SRH | Programme Policy Officer (Resilience); Programme Policy Officer (Emergencies) | 103,000 | $375,000 |
| UNICEF | WASH | Distribution and replenishment of household water treatment units; Procurement and distribution of water tanks to selected households; Conduct hygiene promotion, community mobilization and distribution of water purification tablets | 12,600 | $700,000 |
| UNICEF | Nutrition | Provision of supplementary treatment rations to children aged 6–59 months affected by SAM | 5,371 | $300,000 |
| UNICEF | MPC | Distribution of MPCA to selected beneficiaries | 153,560 | $6,020,000 |
| WFP | Nutrition | Procure and dispatch specialized nutrition commodities; Children aged 6–59 months on BSFP; receive specialized nutritious foods that treat MAM | 13,555 | $980,000 |
| Total | | | | $12,000,000 |

AHF funded activities table (partial, indicative funding**):
- Health & Nutrition: $2m (referral for obstetrical complications; Mobile Health & Nutrition Teams; screening & treatment of acute malnutrition; counselling; support to Therapeutic Feeding Units)
- WASH: $1.5m (safe water storage; household harvesting; groundwater recharge; sanitation & hygiene promotion; water management for livestock)
- Protection: $0.5m (distribution of dignity kits; documentation for women returnees & female-headed HH; referral to essential services)
- Emergency Agriculture: $0.5m (seeds of rain-fed agricultural crops)
- Multi-Sector: $5m (single tranche MPCA; top-up for WHH and PwD)

--- page 9 ---
Main point: Pre-agreed financing details — CERF and AHF disbursement conditions, learning and evaluation plan.

CERF: pre-agreed $12M allocation; disbursed on no-regrets basis when trigger reached; three disbursement conditions (RC/HC endorsement, CERF agency endorsement, ERC activation).
AHF: $10M allocation; aims to mitigate impacts before/during trigger; 10 NGOs (5 international, 5 national) pre-identified.
Both funds: strategically aligned under Revised AA Framework ensuring harmonized geographic prioritisation, synchronized timelines, coordinated partner engagement.

Learning and monitoring: each agency uses existing M&E; joint PDM conducted; PDM data shared across all framework partners; broader/coordinated impact evaluation planned for 2026 (FAO, from late 2025).

Framework valid: 2026–2027 initial period, with option to improve for 2027.

--- page 10 ---
Main point: Technical note on trigger methodology — Window A/B model training, CDI cross-validation, and core principles.

Window A (March): ECMWF SEAS5 forecast precipitation for MAM < 129mm = 1-in-6-year deficit.
Window B (April): Optimized CDI threshold = 0.503; CDI is z-score-standardized composite; cross-validated F1 = 0.82 identifying 9 of 10 historical drought years with 2 false activations.
Monitoring: CHD automated pipeline retrieves ECMWF SEAS5 forecasts in March and observational CDI inputs in April.

Component weights derived from 42 years of historical data (1984–2025). Drought years defined as those where FAO end-of-season Agricultural Stress Index (ASI) exceeds empirical 1-in-4-year return period.
For Window A: tested multiple SEAS5 configurations; pre-season snowcover, soil moisture, and VHI indicators did not improve forecast.
For Window B CDI: iterating over all possible weight combinations at 5% intervals.

Core principles referenced: Prioritizing multi-sectoral response, gender equality, accountability to affected populations, protection from SEAH, supporting localisation, engaging in partnerships.

--- page 11 ---
Main point: Continuation of core principles — narrative text only, no figure.

narrative text, no figure

Three additional principles listed:
- Generating development and climate adaptation co-benefits: AA financing to complement development financing, reduce suffering, address root causes, support climate adaptation.
- Capturing learning: MEL embedded at every phase of AA framework development and implementation.
- Striving for integration: integrate AA activities into existing humanitarian architecture and foster linkages with long-term programming (resilience and development).
